rows.v: The most recognized Italian pasta — long, round strands of durum-wheat semolina. The everyday backbone of Italian home cooking.
Layered baked pasta — flat sheets stacked with sauce, cheese, and often ragù or béchamel. A festive, Sunday-table classic.
Flat ribbon egg pasta, wider than tagliatelle; Roman, classic with butter and Parmigiano.
Quill-shaped tubes with ridges that grab sauce; the workhorse of baked and tossed dishes.
Stuffed pasta pillows — fillings range from ricotta-spinach to meat or squash.
Soft potato-and-flour dumplings; pillowy, served with butter-sage or tomato.
Small ring-shaped stuffed pasta from Emilia, traditionally in capon broth (in brodo).
'Little ears' from Puglia; ideal with broccoli rabe and anchovy.
Thick hollow spaghetti; the canonical shape for amatriciana.
Hand-rolled thick Tuscan noodles, like fat ungainly spaghetti; classic with cacio e pepe.
The defining Neapolitan pizza — tomato, mozzarella, basil. Said to honor Queen Margherita with the colors of the Italian flag.
Naples' purist pie — tomato, garlic, oregano, oil; no cheese.
Folded, sealed pizza turnover, typically stuffed with ricotta and salumi.
Olive-oil-rich flatbread, dimpled and salted; Ligurian staple.
Thin Romagnol griddle flatbread, folded around cured meats and squacquerone.
Crisp Turinese breadsticks, snapped at the table.
The foundational tomato sauce — tomatoes, garlic, olive oil, basil or oregano. Simple, bright, and the base for countless dishes.
The famous green sauce — basil, pine nuts, garlic, Parmigiano/Pecorino, and olive oil, pounded together raw.
Slow-cooked meat ragù from Bologna; traditionally with tagliatelle, never spaghetti.
Simple bright tomato sauce with basil and oil — the foundation sauce.
'Angry' tomato sauce spiked with peperoncino; Roman, with penne.
Garlic gently fried in olive oil with chili — minimalist, fast, spaghetti.
Guanciale, tomato, Pecorino; from Amatrice, classic on bucatini.
Egg, Pecorino, guanciale, black pepper — no cream; emulsified by pasta water.
Tomato, olives, capers, anchovy, garlic — pungent and Neapolitan.
Pecorino Romano and black pepper emulsified with starchy water — three ingredients.
Creamy simmered rice — short-grain rice cooked slowly with broth until it releases its starch into a silky body.
Saffron-gold risotto finished with butter and Parmigiano; the Osso Buco partner.
Mushroom risotto, often porcini, earthy and creamy.
Fried stuffed rice balls from Sicily — saffron rice around ragù or mozzarella.
Venetian rice-and-peas, looser than risotto, a spring dish.
Breaded cutlet baked with tomato sauce and melted cheese. Hugely popular abroad; rooted in the Italian *parmigiana* tradition.
Braised veal shank in white wine and broth; served with gremolata and Milanese risotto.
Veal scaloppine with prosciutto and sage, pan-fried — 'jumps in the mouth.'
Pan-seared cutlets in lemon-butter-caper sauce.
Thick T-bone of Chianina beef, grilled rare over wood.
Bone-in veal cutlet, breaded and fried in butter.
Cold sliced veal under a creamy tuna-caper sauce; Piedmontese.
Beef braised long in red wine (often Barolo) until spoon-tender.
Grilled bread rubbed with garlic and olive oil — the simplest antipasto, often topped with tomato and basil.
Italy's iconic dry-cured ham — salted and air-dried for months to a silky, sweet-savory slice.
Mozzarella, tomato, basil, oil — the colors of the flag; from Capri.
Raw beef sliced paper-thin with oil, lemon, Parmigiano; invented at Harry's Bar.
Fresh mozzarella pouch filled with stracciatella and cream; oozes when cut.
Spreadable spicy fermented pork sausage from Calabria.
Crunchy pickled vegetables; antipasto and condiment.
The famous coffee-and-mascarpone dessert — espresso-soaked ladyfingers layered with sweet mascarpone cream and cocoa.
Italian frozen custard/sorbet — denser and lower in fat than ice cream, served warmer so flavors read intensely.
'Cooked cream' set with gelatin; silky, with berry or caramel.
Crisp fried Sicilian shells filled with sweet ricotta.
Vanilla gelato 'drowned' in a shot of hot espresso.
Warm whipped custard of egg yolks, sugar, and Marsala.
Neapolitan shell pastry of crisp layered dough with citrus-ricotta.
Tall airy Milanese Christmas bread with candied fruit, long-leavened.
'To the tooth' — pasta (or rice) cooked until just firm at the center, never mushy. The single most important Italian cooking rule.
The aromatic base — onion, carrot, celery slowly sweated in oil; start of countless dishes.
Finishing risotto off-heat with cold butter and cheese for creaminess.
Slow moist cooking in liquid that turns tough cuts tender (brasato, osso buco).
Fresh egg pasta of soft flour and eggs; the northern tradition (tagliatelle, ravioli).
'The little shoe' — mopping the last sauce with bread; affectionate, not rude.
